title: Kulajda
subtitle: Czech creamy mushroom soup with dill and egg
excerpt: A thick, cream-enriched soup of mushrooms and potato with a sharp dill and vinegar finish, served with a poached egg floated on top. The definitive Bohemian soup, rich, sour, and like nothing else in European cooking.
sourceNotes: Kulajda is a South Bohemian soup with a distinctive character: it is simultaneously rich (cream), earthy (mushrooms), sharp (vinegar and dill), and filling (potato). The combination is unusual: most cream soups do not include vinegar, but it is precisely the acidity that lifts the dish and prevents it from being heavy. The poached egg floated on top at serving is traditional and adds both richness and visual drama. The soup is associated with the Šumava region of South Bohemia.

content.text: Kulajda works because of three things in tension: the cream gives richness, the vinegar cuts through it, and the dill ties both together. Get that balance right and the soup is extraordinary. Use too little vinegar and it is just a mushroom cream soup; use too much and the cream tastes curdled. Add the vinegar gradually, tasting after each addition.

The poached egg is traditional and worth doing. A soft egg lowered into the bowl just before serving adds richness when the yolk breaks into the cream. Poach the eggs separately in a pan of barely simmering water, not directly in the soup.

items.name: Dried porcini
items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml warm water for 20 minutes

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: peeled and diced into 1.5 cm cubes

content.text: If using dried {{mushrooms-porcini-dried}}, lift them from their soaking liquid, chop roughly, and strain the liquid. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large saucepan over medium-high heat. Add the {{mushrooms-button}} and cook for 5 minutes until golden and all moisture has evaporated. Pour in the {{stock-vegetable}} and, if using porcini, add the chopped mushrooms and strained soaking liquid. Add the {{potato}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{allspice}}. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes until the potato is tender.

Remove the bay leaves and allspice. Stir in the {{double-cream}} and return to a gentle simmer. Add the {{cider-vinegar}} a tablespoon at a time, tasting after each addition, the soup should be pleasantly sharp. Add most of the {{dill}}, reserving a little for serving. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. If poaching {{eggs}}: bring a separate small pan of water to a barely simmering state with a splash of vinegar, swirl gently, and lower in a cracked egg. Poach for 3 minutes. Ladle the soup into bowls, place a poached egg in each, and scatter the remaining dill over.

items.fix: Reduce the heat to a gentle simmer before adding the cream. If it splits, blend a portion of the soup and stir back in, this will partially emulsify the mixture
items.cause: Soup boiling vigorously when cream was added

items.fix: Add more vinegar by the teaspoon until the richness is cut. The vinegar is what makes the soup light despite the cream
items.cause: Too much cream or not enough vinegar

items.fix: Cook the button mushrooms until completely dry and golden before adding stock. The dried porcini (even a small amount) transform the depth of the soup
items.cause: Not cooked long enough before adding liquid, or only button mushrooms with no dried porcini
items.symptom: Mushrooms tasteless in the finished soup

content.text: Kulajda is a South Bohemian regional soup that has become one of the best-known Czech dishes outside the country. It turns up on the menus of Czech restaurants and on the tables of Czech families. Many cook it as a weekend soup, using foraged or dried mushrooms and the cream from the fridge. The mix of cream, vinegar, and dill in a mushroom soup surprises anyone who has not met it before, and pleases anyone who has.
